message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>BACKGROUND</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To deal with pandemics, evaluating the temporal status of an outbreak is important. However, prevailing standards in this respect are mostly empirical and arbitrary. As an alternative, we focus on a novel approach which configures indicators that evaluate topological threats to populations due to the COVID-19 pandemic.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>METHODS</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    We extended the current PzDom model to calculate a threshold of the model for accelerated growth, an indicator of growth extent Re(
                    <jats:italic>v</jats:italic>
                    ), covariance Re(
                    <jats:italic>s</jats:italic>
                    ), a topological number
                    <jats:italic>E</jats:italic>
                    (
                    <jats:italic>l</jats:italic>
                    ), and expected sums of possibly increasing numbers of infected people. We term this the exPzDom model.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>RESULTS</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The indicators in the exPzDom model adhere well to the empirical dynamics of SARS-CoV-2 infected people and align appropriately with actual policies instituted by the Japanese government.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>CONCLUSIONS</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The described indicators could be leveraged pursuant of objective evaluation based on mathematics. Further testing of the reliability and robustness of exPzDom model in other pandemic contexts is warranted.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Novel indicators for evaluating topological threats to populations from pandemics applied to COVID-19

reference.unstructured: Adachi S . Exploring group theory and topology for analyzing the structure of biological hierarchies. arXiv:1603.00959v8 [q-bio.PE] 2019a. https://arxiv.org/abs/1603.00959

reference.article-title: Rigid geometry solves “curse of dimensionality” effects in clustering methods: An application to omics data
